sentences.text: Fine, so school.
I started to tell you I had my confrontations because of-- it was more of my father's religious belief.
I loved and respected my father, so I did not want to do anything that could have caused any concern with him.
And I also was concerned about the belief of my older brother.
My older brother was even more religious than my father, and he spent an inordinately large amount of time in learning.
And sometimes he corrected my father.
And, of course, he went through school without ever writing, and these things were important.
So in these episodes there was a continuously increasing pressure that I should write.
This teacher made it a program of his, and I tried to conform with his requests.
For example, he asked me to answer some questions.
We did that in front of the class.
There were some questions asked, four or five students before every hour.
And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for example, he tried to catch me in any inaccuracies.
I remember distinctly, in one of them, he was wrong.
So I told him that, sir, you must be right, but in our textbook in page 128, the 42nd line, it is written differently, and this is why I did not answer correctly.
And the class was laughing, because they knew this, and, of course, regardless who they were, in this context, they were on my side almost unanimously.
But at the end of fourth year, he called in my father and told him that the balance of power is shifting.
It is true that the director of the school, [?
Palfy ?], was protecting me, but now the district director became [INAUDIBLE], who was another fascist.
And he has his word that if I don't write next year, they will not allow me to continue.
At that time in Hungary, if I did not get further, there was no school that would have accepted me.
Already Jewish students could not get into public schools.
They could only go to the Jewish school, but there was only one Jewish gymnasium in Budapest, and there was a-- a much larger number of applicant.
There were three schools in Budapest where they had Jewish classes, but these were separate as well.
So he called in my father and told him that, don't you think, Mr.
Cohen, that, in this day and age, in the year-- and this was in 1938-- it is anachronistic for a Jew not to write on Saturday when there are all these Jewish laws?
And my father told him that I-- in my consideration, the Jewish laws are anachronistic, which made him to lose his patience.
He said, regardless what you think, if he's not writing, then he will not be accepted.
And my father called me in to his room for a discussion.
He said a very important thing he wants to discuss with me.
He said that he does not want to advise me, because it's up to my conscience, but he quoted different Talmudic quotations that, in case of the danger of life, the laws can be transgressed.

And he said that, although this does not provide immediate danger, in a broad interpretation, if you are not studying and if you cannot finish your studies, then eventually you cannot make a living, then this is endangering you life.
But he doesn't want to advise me.
But if I want to write, then he will not object.
And of course, this was a great relief for me, because I always wanted to write.
And I did not think that this would be a major difference between me and God.
And so I told him that, considering the circumstances, I am willing to write.
And I looked very sad, but in fact I was very happy that this, which I considered was basically an ordeal, was over.
Because it's difficult to be so different.
Because in the class we were 49 students.
Out of these 49, about 22 were Jewish.
But everybody wrote.
In the whole school, I was the only one, after my brother left.
So-- and my younger brother, but he was in a Jewish class and they were teaching differently.
But I was the only one in the mainstream in this school.
And I don't know how many others were in Budapest.
So in grade five, this gymnasium, which was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at 15 years of age, I started to write on Saturdays, and I keep writing ever since.
Not necessarily on Saturdays, but I make no difference.
What elements of the Jewish observance remained important to you?
Only those that my father asked me to observe, and I promised him to observe.
I-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] don't really consider the-- the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jewish or any other religion-- the rules as important.
In-- in my opinion, the only thing that counts, not what I pray but what I do.
Nevertheless, I am observing.
My father asked me, so I didn't ask him why.
When he discovered that I am not as religious as he is, he said that, no matter what I think, I should observe [INAUDIBLE] services.
I should [INAUDIBLE] and I should fast in Yom Kippur.
If I do nothing else, at least this I must do.
And I agreed to that.
This was after a crisis in our family, because I was discovered that it was on a Rosh Hashanah, not Yom Kippur, under the prayers book, I was reading another book.
And of course, this [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was the result of my studying.
I had my doubts.
You see, I always, as a child placed myself into another position.
And I told that if I were God, I wouldn't be very much impressed by people telling me how great I am and grant them favor just because they say so.
And so I found the long services a bit boring and I covered the book towards my father.
But a neighbor on the other side, I was very upset at him because he reported me to my father.
At which point, he went to the chief rabbi to Budapest.
He always went to him even before important business decisions.

And I heard what he said, and it was very interesting how [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] strong and considerate our religion is, because he told him that he need not to be concerned, because he did what was his part, that he showed me the right way.
And the rest is in between God and me, and he should rest assured that, when the time comes, I will find the right way.
So at which point, he accepted that.
And I was extremely concerned, not so much about how God will accept or reject my transgression, but about Him.
And from then on until I was in high school, before the Germans came, I always went to him and I was very careful to participate in everything, mainly because of what he felt, not--
No.
That's very interesting.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't really have plans for my future.
See, I was very pessimistic as regards to-- to the war.
Not so much the Germans I was concerned about.
My [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] feeling towards war was of extreme fear.
I never really knew how can one survive a war.
So the only thing that I had elaborate plans for is how to survive the war.
I wasn't really planning anything else.
Yes, actually I was concerned even before.
But at age 10-- I was 10 and 1/2, more than 10-- when the Munich Agreement came and Chamberlain went home and he that we have peace for our time.
My father came home that night and he found me in a rather depressed condition and he asked me what's wrong.
And I told him that, in my opinion the Second World War just broke out.
And until this day, I am puzzled that how, at age 10 and 1/2, I had more insight than the British prime minister.
But that's the way it was.
So I really had no plans any longer than to survive the war.
And again, I never had, in my wildest dreams, anything like this, what has happened.
I was just afraid that the war will come to us and the bombs from airplanes and all these sort of things were very clearly frightening.
I did not complete it because, at grade six, the Germans came in, in 1944, March the 19th.
And shortly thereafter gymnasium stopped.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They closed in April.
I had a major incident with this teacher, who remained the so-called home school teacher.

And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When he gave the final, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] year-end results out, he tried to [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] somehow-- I can't remember very clear details, but the last part of this was that he asked me why I am smiling.
And I wasn't smiling.
I was very depressed because I thought that this is the end of school forever.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he then accused me of lying, that I did not smile.
And he called me out and hit me.
And I hit him back.
I thought that this is the end of anything anyway.
At least I will give it to him.
And at age 16, as we were, I think I was stronger than he was.
But the class stopped me.
And he immediately took me to the director of the school, who was the same as the one that had protected me earlier, and he asked me-- didn't ask him, he asked me what has happened.
And I told him.
And he told this teacher that, if there's any continuation of this, he personally will make it sure that he will regret it for the rest of his life.
There was no continuation at that time.
It stopped with the director of the school.
And I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] felt satisfaction, that at least a little bit, I gave back.
With school closed, the next thing that happened that the Germans started to organize the so-called Jewish councils.
My religious teacher, Adolf Fish, who was a rabbi, became the organizer of the messenger service of the Jewish council.
Yes, Judenrat.
And I joined that group, and had some interesting-- I would say, at that time, horrifying experiences-- which made me to leave that council to have a month or so.

Eichmann, who arrived to Budapest, demanded from the Judenrat [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] payment of-- and I don't remember the exact amount-- payment of a large sum of American dollars and a large amount of gold.
And the Judenrat sent me with the letter, since I was the runner of the Judenrat, sent me with the letter that they only have half of what he wanted.

And he went into a temper tantrum that it was very frightening, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wrote a letter back, and also stated that he will arrest the whole council if they don't get the whole amount that he wanted.

So my next return with the letter that they have the whole amount was the last thing that I did for them, because he became even more angry, and I was afraid that he's going to become even more angry or do something.
But he didn't.
He just sent me-- gave me another letter, and I don't know what was in there, because after that I resigned from that position and went to work in a brick factory.
And I worked there until the middle of June.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The invasion was on June the 6th.
I was still working in the factory, but shortly thereafter, there were movements-- military movements in our area-- which we could not explain why.
Later, we learned that Hungarian fascists mobilized approximately 40,000 gendarme to force the deportation of the Jews from Budapest.
By that time, all the other Jews were deported, but Governor [?
Horthy, ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] learning what has happened to the Jews, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] said that no more.
And these 40,000 people were attempting to force him.
But by that time, he regained his balance and he mobilized his own troops, approximately 5,000, very well armed, mainly officers, and sent them out.
And at the same time, he sent out trucks for the Jews in factories in that part of Budapest, which was at the outskirts, since, in the other outskirts, the gendarme got the Jews and deported them.
So this was mainly a defensive action that they took us to the Hungarian Army from that place, and this is how I became a member of the Hungarian Army in this paramilitary unit at age 16.
Because this was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] under normal circumstances, it's only age 18 they took the people in.
What was the name of this paramilitary organization?
The Hungarian name was [HUNGARIAN], which I-- if I translate into English, it was Labor Service.
They were military units in the sense that they were belonging to the Hungarian Army.
We got the same ration as the members of the army, and we got even a small salary that went to those people.
And we got cigarettes, which we did not use at the time, and the same rations as the others.
The name of this unit-- they had the ordinary names that the Army labeled their units, and I was in the Unit 101 per 605.
And I was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there until I left this unit in October, 1944.
In Budapest.
In my own home.
My brother was in this military unit, and because he was four years older, he was 19-- between 19 and 20-- or 20-- and he was in this military, or paramilitary unit.
My mother, my younger brother, my father, and sister were in a so-called Jewish house.
In order to prepare deportation, the Jews were first concentrated in Jewish houses.
These Jewish houses were all over the city, and it was difficult to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] know why they started, but they did not start with a ghetto.
This was a [INAUDIBLE] transition, and in houses which were not designated Jewish houses, the people had to leave and double up in apartments.
This was still fairly comfortable.
In our apartment, we had to take in the younger brother of my father and his wife, and a sister of my mother.
But we still were fairly comfortable in the Jewish house.
And this is how they lived.
They-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the situation was gradually worse and worse.
They-- for example, once they concentrated the Jews in these so-called Jewish houses, they limited their permission to go out of these houses to two or three hours a day, during the day.
So, while initially I lived at home and worked in this big factory, in order to continue to work I had to move into the factory, because there was no way that I could commute because of the house.
And this episode that the army took me in as a sort of rescue operation came at the end of this.
But it's an interesting example of how some people were more sympathetic, just to illustrate you this point.
At one time, the factory decided to go on strike.
There were about 400 workers in the factory.
The organizer of the strike, who was either a social democrat or a communist, told us not to participate.
He said that you report on work.
He said that they-- if you go on strike, you will have far much more trouble than you could cope with.
And they cannot do anything with the two of you.
So we reported to work.
And they gave us work.
We were cleaning different areas.
But of course the factory did not move while the laborers were on strike.
Money.
They wanted to have higher salaries.
Even if they were not hostile to Jews, everybody supported-- almost everybody-- the war efforts.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because they may not have liked the Germans so much, but they were more afraid of the Russians than the Germans.
So they were not striking for political reasons.
No.
As the Germans came in approximately a month later, all Jewish properties were confiscated, so his store as well.
His store was confiscated, and there was a-- it was sealed by the Ministry of Jewish Properties.
And it wasn't given to anybody.
It was there, sealed.
We subsequently-- later on in my hiding, in our own house, because I copied the seal that they sealed the doorways.
And once I left the military unit, I was hiding in a room in our own apartment which was sealed from outside by the Ministry of Jewish Properties.
And this was always, when they were searching the apartment, they always went by because--
I needed help to that, so whenever I went in there-- I was the only one who was an escapee from the army, so the others could be there legally, and somebody put the seal on.
I had several seals, which were basically very primitive.
It was a brown paper that was with some adhesives on one side, and a signature of the ministry's employee was on it, which I could easily imitate.
So I had several of them ready.
And there was a sign that it's sealed by the Ministry of Jewish Properties.
And this was really a storage space for the-- for the business in our apartment, and I was there, under books.
Underneath.
Yeah.
There was a hole prepared for me on the book shelf, and the books were on it and I went underneath.
I pulled the bookshelf on myself, just in case if they look into the room, but they never did.
As long as it was necessary.
One hour, two hours, three hours, when they had searches in Jewish apartments.
This was near the ghetto.
This was after the Hungarian fascist leader took over the power from Hungarian governors.
After October the 15th.
Yes.
Actually, that was one of the first [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that they introduced after the Germans came in.
And in the army, I had to wear yellow arm bands.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So these [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were occasionally the source of unpleasant episodes.
There were active fascists who [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were very hostile towards Jews, who were [INAUDIBLE] us.
But basically the main bulk of the population ignored it.
They were not really hostile.
They were not friendly.
I think that most of them agreed what is happening, but they didn't want to actively participate in it.
So once the Germans were in, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we lived-- my family lived under these conditions.
Now, our military unit, on the other hand, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] got into trouble the way as I expected it, not with the Germans but with the Allies.
I'm going to interrupt you so that we can take a short break.
